preamble: Rhotacism is a well-documented historical sound change where an alveolar fricative, typically the voiceless alveolar fricative /s/, transforms into a rhotic consonant, most commonly an alveolar flap [ɾ]. This process is observed across various language families and historical periods.
phoneticEffects: This transformation is a form of lenition, characterized by articulatory weakening, reduced duration, and an increase in sonority

examples.note: Intervocalic /s/ in the nominative singular shifts to /ɾ/ in the genitive singular.
Intervocalic /s/ in the nominative singular shifts to /ɾ/ in the genitive singular.
Intervocalic /s/ in the infinitive shifts to /ɾ/ in the past plural.
Intervocalic /s/ in the infinitive shifts to /ɾ/ in the past plural.
In some dialects, syllable-coda /s/ is replaced with an alveolar flap [ɾ] before certain consonants.

sources: Romero, J., & Martin, S. (2003). Articulatory Weakening as Basis of Historical Rhotacism. In *Proceedings of the 15th International Congress of Phonetic Sciences (ICPhS-15)*, 2825-2828.
